title: Did the Prophets Teach Pure Monotheism?
subtitle: Examining Tawhid, the Shema, and Christian Trinitarian Theology
summary: Islamic and Christian traditions make competing claims about what the biblical prophets—especially Abraham, Moses, and Jesus—taught regarding the nature of God. Muslims assert that all prophets preached Tawhid (pure, uncompromising monotheism), while Christians argue that Trinitarian truth was revealed typologically and eschatologically. This article examines scriptural evidence from both traditions, their theological frameworks, and how each interprets the historical claims of the other.

seo.metaTitle: Did Prophets Teach Pure Monotheism? Islamic Tawhid vs. Christian Trinity
seo.metaDescription: Examine how Islam and Christianity interpret prophetic teaching on monotheism, from the Shema to Jesus' prayers, and what each tradition claims about God's nature.

sections.title: Introduction: Two Interpretative Frameworks
sections.body: The question of whether the prophets taught pure monotheism—understood in Islamic theology as Tawhid (التوحيد)—sits at the heart of Islamic-Christian theological disagreement. Both traditions claim ancient prophetic authority. Islam maintains that all prophets from Adam through Muhammad consistently preached Tawhid: the absolute oneness and uniqueness of God (Allah), with no partners, no tripartite nature, no incarnation. Christianity, particularly Trinitarian Christianity, argues that the prophets of Israel were divinely guided toward an understanding of God's triune nature—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—which was fully revealed in Jesus Christ. This article examines the scriptural basis for each position, the internal theological logic of each tradition, and the genuine points of scriptural tension that fuel the debate.

sections.title: Islamic Perspective: Tawhid Across All Prophets
sections.body:
In Islamic theology, Tawhid (التوحيد)—the assertion of God's absolute oneness—is the foundational obligation and primary message of all prophets. The Qur'an repeatedly emphasizes that every prophet from Noah to Muhammad was sent with the same core message: worship none but Allah alone. This is not merely numerical monotheism (belief in one God rather than many) but qualitative monotheism—the affirmation that God has no partners (شريك Sharik), no offspring, no resemblance to creation, and that all worship, devotion, and obedience belong to Him exclusively.

Islamic exegetes point to several lines of evidence. First, the Qur'an portrays the patriarchs themselves teaching strict monotheism to their descendants. Abraham (Ibrahim), whom both traditions revere, is explicitly depicted instructing his sons in Tawhid. Second, the foundational prayer of Israel—the Shema—is read by Muslim scholars as an affirmation of numerical and categorical oneness that stands in tension with later Christian claims about Trinity. Third, Jesus himself in the Gospels is portrayed praying to God (Dua), which Muslims argue would be incomprehensible if Jesus himself were divine or co-equal with God. The Islamic interpretation holds that the doctrine of Trinity emerged as a deviation from the pure monotheistic message that the prophets themselves taught and that Jesus consistently reiterated.

sections.title: Christian Perspective: Monotheism and Trinitarian Development
sections.body:
Mainstream Christian theology—particularly Trinitarian Christianity, which includes Roman Catholicism, Eastern Orthodoxy, and most Protestant denominations—affirms that God is absolutely one in substance and nature (homoousios) while existing as three distinct persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. This is not seen as a departure from monotheism but as the full revelation of what biblical monotheism entails. Christian interpreters argue that the Old Testament prophets, while affirming God's oneness (which Christians fully embrace), operated within a pre-Incarnational framework. The Incarnation of Christ—God becoming human in Jesus—is understood as the eschatological revelation of God's eternal nature, not a contradiction of earlier monotheistic teaching.

Christian exegetes note that the Old Testament contains internal features Christians interpret as hints toward Trinitarian revelation: references to God's Spirit, Wisdom personified, the Son, and repeated passages where God refers to Himself in plural ("Let us make man in our image"). The New Testament, in this reading, makes explicit what was latent in the Old. Jesus is understood as the divine Logos (Word) through whom all creation was made and sustained. When Jesus prayed to the Father, Christian theology explains this as reflecting His voluntary submission and incarnate humility, not His metaphysical subordination or non-divinity. The Trinity is presented as the deepest truth about God's nature, fully consistent with monotheism because all three persons share one divine essence.

sections.title: Scriptural Testimony: Core Passages on God's Oneness
sections.body:
Both traditions point to the same scriptural sources as anchors for monotheism, though they interpret them differently. The Islamic reading emphasizes the clarity of numerical and exclusive oneness. The Christian reading incorporates these affirmations while arguing they are consistent with Trinitarian theology.

The Shema—"Hear, O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one"—stands as the cardinal declaration of Jewish monotheism and is cited by Jesus himself in the Gospels as the greatest commandment. In Islamic interpretation, this verse is taken at face value: God is one, with no plurality, no Trinity. In Christian interpretation, this verse affirms God's uniqueness and oneness of purpose while not precluding the threefold personhood revealed in Christ.

The Qur'an's emphatic declarations of Tawhid are similarly read by Muslims as direct affirmations of pure monotheism that stand in implicit tension with Christian theology. Surah Al-Ikhlas (Chapter 112), which Muslims recite daily in prayer, asserts: "Say, 'He is Allah, [who is] One." Muslims understand this as categorical: God is one, without partnership, without offspring, without equals. Similarly, the declaration in Surah Al-Baqarah (2:163) states unambiguously: "And your god is one God. There is no deity [worthy of worship] except Him, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful."

Christians, while affirming these statements as truths about God, interpret them within the framework of Trinitarian monotheism—one God, three persons, one essence. The New Testament passages affirming Jesus' divinity (such as John 1:1, "the Word was God") are understood by Christians as expanding on and clarifying what biblical monotheism includes. Muslims interpret these same passages as later theological developments inconsistent with Jesus' own teaching.

sections.title: Abraham, Moses, and Jesus: What Did They Teach?
sections.body:
The question of what the patriarchs and prophets explicitly taught is central to the debate. Islamic scholars argue that when Abraham is depicted in the Qur'an at the moment of death instructing his children, he emphasizes pure monotheism. His children respond: "We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham and Ishmael and Isaac—one God. And we are Muslims [in submission] to Him." The phrase "one God" (إلاهاً واحداً Ilahan Wahida) is read by Muslims as unambiguous: a single deity with no complexity, division, or trinitarian nature.

Christian interpreters note that while the patriarchs taught belief in the one God of Israel, they operated within the progressive revelation model—they knew God was one, but did not know the full nature of that oneness as Trinitarian. This parallels how the patriarchs knew God would send a Messiah but did not know all details of His coming. The fact that Abraham, Moses, and Jesus affirm God's oneness, in this reading, is perfectly compatible with the Incarnation and Trinity being true developments of that oneness.

Regarding Jesus specifically, the Gospels depict Him enforcing the Shema as the greatest commandment (Mark 12:29), teaching that eternal life consists in knowing "the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ" (John 17:3), and regularly praying to God the Father. Muslims understand these passages as Jesus clearly distinguishing himself from God and affirming monotheism in its pure form. Christians interpret the same passages as Jesus, in his incarnate form, maintaining communion with the Father (as the Son must) while simultaneously embodying divine nature. The prayer of Jesus to the Father is understood by Christians as the incarnate Christ's filial relationship to God, expressing His human submission and dependence, consistent with His full divinity.

sections.title: Jesus' Claims and Prayers: A Point of Interpretative Tension
sections.body:
Few passages generate more theological heat than Jesus' statements about his own nature and his prayers to God. The Gospels present a complex picture that each tradition reads coherently but differently.

Muslim exegetes emphasize Jesus' repeated prayers to God, his statements that he possesses no independent power, and his clear subordination of himself to God. In John 17:3, Jesus defines eternal life as knowing "the only true God"—a definition that many Muslims argue would be strange if Jesus himself were truly God. Similarly, in John 5:30, Jesus states, "I can do nothing by myself." In John 14:28, Jesus says, "the Father is greater than I." Muslim scholars conclude that these statements reflect Jesus' actual nature: he was a righteous servant and messenger, not divine. The later Christian interpretation of Jesus' divinity, in this view, represents a theological development post-Jesus, perhaps influenced by Greco-Roman philosophical categories foreign to Jesus' Jewish monotheistic context.

Christian interpreters acknowledge these passages but understand them within Christological frameworks developed early in the Christian movement. Jesus' prayers to the Father are understood as reflecting His incarnate existence—He emptied Himself (kenosis, Philippians 2:7) to take human form and thus genuinely lived a human life of prayer, obedience, and dependence on God, even while maintaining His divine nature. His statement that "the Father is greater than I" can mean the Father is greater in position or authority, not necessarily in metaphysical being, especially given Jesus' incarnate condition. Meanwhile, other Gospel passages are read by Christians as making divine claims: "I am" statements (John 8:58), "I and the Father are one" (John 10:30), and the prologue to John's Gospel asserting that the Logos was with God and was God.

What neither tradition disputes is that Jesus emphasized monotheism, prayed to God, and urged total devotion to the one God. The disagreement centers on how to synthesize this emphasis on monotheism with the New Testament's assertions about Jesus' divinity and resurrection.

sections.title: Pauline Theology and the Cosmic Christ
sections.body:
A significant source of Islamic-Christian debate is the theology of Paul, especially in passages like 1 Corinthians 8:4-6 and Colossians 1:15-17. Muslims often argue that Paul, not Jesus, introduced the concept that elevated Jesus to divinity, departing from the original Abrahamic monotheism.

In 1 Corinthians 8:4-6, Paul writes that "there is no other God but one" and references "one God, the Father, of whom are all things" and "one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things." Muslims argue this clearly subordinates Jesus to God and maintains strict monotheism—there is one God (the Father), and Jesus is identified as a separate entity (Lord) through whom things were made. Christian interpreters respond that the passage does not resolve the metaphysical relationship between the one God (Father) and the one Lord (Jesus Christ) in the direction Muslims assume, and argue that Paul here is addressing paganism and asserting monotheism against polytheism, not precluding Jesus' divinity; indeed, Jesus is identified as the cosmic mediator through whom all things exist.

Colossians 1:15-17 is similarly interpreted by Christians as Christologically loaded: Jesus is "the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation," and "by him all things were created...He is before all things, and in him all things are held together." Christians read this as exalting Jesus to divine status—he is the visible expression of God, the sustainer of creation. Muslims argue this language, while elevated, portrays Jesus as created ("firstborn of all creation") and as a mediating instrument through which God acts, but not as God Himself or as uncreated.

The historical question is complex: did Paul introduce new theology not present in Jesus' teaching, or did Paul articulate the implications of Jesus' Resurrection and exaltation? Scholars across both traditions debate this. Muslim scholars typically argue for discontinuity (Paul innovated). Christian scholars typically argue for continuity (Paul unpacked what the Resurrection revealed). What is clear is that by the late first century, Pauline churches were worshipping Jesus and ascribing cosmic significance to him, a development that Islamic theology rejects as theological drift from monotheism.

sections.title: Trinity as Theological Development: Christian Logic
sections.body:
The doctrine of the Trinity was formally articulated over centuries, reaching its classical expression in the Nicene Creed (325 CE) and the Definition of Chalcedon (451 CE). This raises a legitimate historical question: if Jesus taught pure monotheism and if the apostles believed monotheism, how did Trinitarian theology develop?

Christian historians and theologians offer several answers. First, they argue that the Resurrection of Jesus created a theological crisis for Jewish monotheists. If Jesus, whom many believed to be the Messiah, had risen from the dead and appeared to them, what did this mean about God's nature? The early Jerusalem church remained robustly Jewish and monotheistic, but they found themselves worshipping the risen Jesus. Second, the reflection on Jesus' pre-existence (suggested by passages like John 1:1 and Colossians 1:15-17) led Christians to ask: who was Jesus before the Incarnation? Third, the theology of the Holy Spirit—understood as God's active presence—raised questions about personhood and distinction within God. Over time, Christian theologians concluded that the best way to maintain monotheism while affirming the Resurrection, Jesus' pre-existence, and the Spirit's personhood was the doctrine of Trinity: one God, eternally existing as three persons in perfect unity.

This development is viewed by Christians as organic development of revelation, analogous to how the Old Testament itself contains development in God's self-revelation across different historical periods. Muslims, by contrast, argue that the Trinity represents a departure from the original monotheistic message—it is a theological innovation introduced by later generations, influenced by Greco-Roman philosophy, and inconsistent with what the prophets actually taught.

sections.title: Islamic Perspective: The Case for Prophetic Monotheism
sections.body:
Islamic theology offers a comprehensive critique of the Christian Trinity claim, asserting that the historical and scriptural evidence supports pure monotheism as the prophetic message. Several arguments are central:

1. Textual Clarity: The Qur'an makes unambiguous statements denying any possibility of partnership with God or offspring. Surah Al-Maa'idah (5:73) explicitly addresses the Christian claim: "They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the third of three." And there is no god except one God. And if they do not desist from what they are saying, there will surely afflict the disbelievers among them a painful punishment." Islamic scholars argue this verse directly addresses and rejects Christian Trinitarian theology, indicating that such theology was known in Arabia and explicitly rejected by the Qur'an. Surah An-Nisaa (4:171) similarly addresses Christians, correcting what the Qur'an views as their distortion: "Do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him."

2. Prophetic Consistency: Islamic theology asserts that all prophets taught the same essential message and faced similar rejection. Abraham preached Tawhid and was persecuted. Moses taught God's oneness and faced resistance. Jesus taught monotheism and was rejected. The idea that they taught different doctrines contradicts the Islamic principle of prophetic consistency.

3. Historical Suspicion: Islamic scholars argue that Trinitarian theology emerged centuries after Jesus, was shaped by Greek philosophical categories foreign to the Jewish environment of Jesus and the early apostles, and cannot be attributed to Jesus or his immediate followers. The Gospel of Mark, thought by many scholars to be the earliest Gospel, makes no explicit claim that Jesus is divine; later Gospels (particularly John) make higher Christological claims, suggesting development over time.

4. Logical Coherence: Muslims argue that the Trinity is logically incoherent—claiming to be monotheism while affirming three distinct persons as God is contradictory. While Christian theologians define Trinity carefully (three persons, one essence/substance), Muslims argue that calling three things one thing while maintaining their distinctness violates the principle of non-contradiction.

5. The Perfect Messenger: Islamic theology holds that Allah would not leave ambiguity about something as fundamental as His nature. If Allah is truly one and has no partners, He would ensure this was clear in the message of His prophets. The universality of the Shema across Judaism and Islam, and Jesus' own emphasis on the oneness of God, are understood as confirmation that this was the prophetic teaching.

sections.title: Christian Perspective: How Trinity Maintains Monotheism
sections.body:
Christian theologians affirm that the Trinity is fully consistent with monotheism and that rejecting it actually misses the depth of biblical revelation. Several counterarguments are central:

1. One God, Three Persons: Trinitarian theology carefully distinguishes between God's essence (ousia in Greek) and God's personhood. There is one God—one divine essence shared by Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. But there are three distinct persons. This is not the same as saying there are three Gods; it is saying there is one God who eternally exists as three. The analogy is imperfect, but Christian theologians compare it to marriage (two persons, one flesh), water (one substance, three states), or mind (one unified consciousness with multiple facets).

2. Biblical Warrant: Christians point to multiple strands of biblical evidence:
   - The plural pronouns used of God in Genesis ("Let us make man in our image")
   - Jesus explicitly calling God "Father" and establishing a filial relationship
   - The Spirit being referred to as a person with will, knowledge, and agency
   - The baptism accounts where Father, Son, and Spirit appear distinct yet simultaneously present
   - Paul's blessing formula ("The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Ghost") presupposing a trinitarian structure

3. Incarnation as Revelation: The Incarnation is understood as the pinnacle of God's self-revelation. Jesus is the "image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15) and the "exact representation of His being" (Hebrews 1:3). In Jesus, the invisible God became visible, the transcendent became immanent, the infinite became finite. This is not a diminishment of monotheism but its fullest expression—God revealing His inner nature (Trinity) to humanity.

4. Addressing Quran 5:73: While Muslims cite this verse as rejection of Christian Trinity, Christian scholars note that the verse specifically rejects "Allah is the third of three"—a modalistic or tritheistic distortion. Classical Trinitarian theology does not teach this; it teaches one God in three persons. The Qur'an may be addressing a heretical form of Christianity, not classical Trinitarian orthodoxy. (Islamic scholars dispute this, arguing that any claim to three divine beings contradicts monotheism by definition.)

5. Development and Consistency: Christian theologians argue that the Trinity is not a foreign imposition but an organic development of Jesus' own teaching and the apostolic witness. When the disciples encountered the Risen Jesus, when they experienced the Spirit at Pentecost, and when they reflected on Jesus' pre-existence, they were driven by the data of revelation to Trinitarian theology. This is not contradiction but deepening understanding.

6. Practical Monotheism: Christians argue that they are as monotheistic in practice and devotion as Muslims. They worship one God, serve one God, acknowledge one God's sovereignty, and submit to one God's will. The Trinity is not a practical polytheism but a metaphysical articulation of how the one God they worship eternally exists.

sections.title: Scholarly and Apologetic Perspectives
sections.body:
Scholars across Islamic and Christian traditions continue to debate this question rigorously. A few scholarly observations are worth noting:

On Christological development, mainstream biblical scholarship (across Christian, Jewish, and secular scholars) generally agrees that the earliest layers of the Jesus tradition (the historical Jesus' own sayings, the earliest Jerusalem church) are characterized by high reverence for Jesus but that the highest Christological claims (Jesus as pre-existent divine Logos, as worthy of worship, as one with the Father) developed over time, reaching their fullest expression in the Gospel of John and later patristic theology. This historical development is acknowledged by Christian scholars and is understood theologically as the Spirit's guidance into fuller truth (John 16:13). Muslim scholars cite this same development as evidence that the Trinity is a later theological innovation, not original teaching.

On the divinity claims, Christian scholars note that early Christian worship practices—calling on Jesus' name, praying to Jesus, confessing "Jesus is Lord" (Kurios in Greek, which carries divine overtones)—are early attestations of high Christology. These practices emerged in thoroughly Jewish contexts (Acts 7:59 depicts Stephen praying to Jesus), which would have been revolutionary if Jesus' followers believed him to be a mere prophet. Muslim scholars argue that early enthusiasm and misunderstanding could account for this without implying Jesus actually taught divinity.

On the Incarnation, Christian scholars emphasize that the classical formulation (Jesus is true God and true man, maintaining two natures in one person) was intended to preserve both the full humanity of Jesus (he was not merely God playing human) and his full divinity. This was formulated to address both docetic heresies (claiming Jesus wasn't really human) and Nestorian heresies (claiming Jesus was two separate persons). Muslim scholarship sometimes interprets this formulation as conceptually incoherent.

On monotheism's definition, scholars across traditions agree that monotheism, at minimum, means rejecting polytheism and affirming God's supremacy and uniqueness. Christian scholars argue their position maintains this; Muslim scholars argue it does not. This represents a genuine philosophical disagreement about what "one God" logically entails, not merely a disagreement about what historical figures taught.

sections.title: Conclusion: Genuine Disagreement, Not Simple Clarity
sections.body:
After examining the scriptural, historical, and theological dimensions of this question, we can conclude that both Islamic and Christian traditions have coherent interpretations of the prophecies and teachings, but they arrive at contradictory conclusions.

The Islamic interpretation is logically straightforward: God is one, without partners, and the prophets consistently taught this. Jesus taught monotheism, prayed to God as a servant prays, and never claimed to be divine. Later generations misunderstood or deliberately corrupted this teaching, introducing the Trinity. The Qur'an explicitly rejects Trinitarian theology as a deviation from pure monotheism.

The Christian interpretation is historically developmental: The early disciples, confronted by the Resurrection and their lived experience of Jesus' continued presence and authority, came to recognize in Jesus the presence of God Himself. The Trinity was not invented but discovered through reflection on what the Resurrection revealed. God is one, but this one God eternally exists as three persons—a mystery beyond human reason but consistent with biblical data and the logic of God's self-revelation in Christ.

Neither tradition can point to a single verse that definitively settles the matter in the way it wishes. The Islamic scholar rightly emphasizes Jesus' prayers and monotheistic emphasis. The Christian scholar rightly notes Jesus' apparent divine claims and the early church's worship practices. Both traditions claim apostolic succession and divine guidance, yet they reach opposite conclusions.

What is clear is that this is not a matter of selective reading or obvious historical fact, but a genuine theological disagreement rooted in different hermeneutical assumptions (how revelation develops over time, what the Resurrection means, what texts are authoritative) and different logical frameworks (what constitutes strict monotheism, how to reconcile biblical diversity, etc.). Honest scholars in both traditions must acknowledge the strength of the other's arguments and the genuine difficulty of the question, even as they maintain their own conviction.

quranVerses.arabic: أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِذْ قَالَ لِبَنِيهِ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِى قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ إِلَٰهَكَ وَإِلَٰهَ ءَابَآئِكَ إِبْرَٰهِۦمَ وَإِسْمَٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَٰقَ إِلَٰهًۭا وَٰحِدًۭا وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Or were you witnesses when death approached Jacob, when he said to his sons, "What will you worship after me?" They said, "We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham and Ishmael and Isaac - one God. And we are Muslims [in submission] to Him."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: شَهِدَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمِ قَآئِمًۢا بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ
quranVerses.translation: Allah witnesses that there is no deity except Him, and [so do] the angels and those of knowledge - [that He is] maintaining [creation] in justice. There is no deity except Him, the Exalted in Might, the Wise.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِلَٰهُكُمْ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ لَّآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ
quranVerses.translation: And your god is one God. There is no deity [worthy of worship] except Him, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا
quranVerses.translation: O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, "Three"; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَٰثَةٍۢ ۘ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّآ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۚ وَإِن لَّمْ يَنتَهُوا۟ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ
quranVerses.translation: They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the third of three." And there is no god except one God. And if they do not desist from what they are saying, there will surely afflict the disbelievers among them a painful punishment.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one.
In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
Jesus answered, “The greatest is, ‘Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one:
This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ.

Therefore concerning the eating of things sacrificed to idols, we know that no idol is anything in the world, and that there is no other God but one. For though there are things that are called “gods”, whether in the heavens or on earth; as there are many “gods” and many “lords”; yet to us there is one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we for him; and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things, and we live through him.

who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation. For by him all things were created, in the heavens and on the earth, things visible and things invisible, whether thrones or dominions or principalities or powers; all things have been created through him, and for him. He is before all things, and in him all things are held together.
Jesus said to him, “‘You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind.’

faq.question: Did Jesus claim to be God?
faq.answer: This is one of the central points of disagreement. Muslims argue the Gospels show Jesus praying to God, claiming to have no independent power, and never explicitly calling himself God. Christians point to passages where Jesus makes statements like 'I and the Father are one' (John 10:30), accepts worship, and applies divine prerogatives to himself. Scholars debate whether Jesus' saying 'I am' (ego eimi) echoes the divine name, and whether later Gospel accounts (especially John) represent Jesus' actual claims or later Christian interpretation. Both traditions agree Jesus spoke with authority and was called by exalted titles, but disagree whether this amounts to claiming divinity.

faq.question: Is the Christian Trinity inconsistent with monotheism?
faq.answer: This depends on how monotheism is defined. Christians argue that affirming one God (one divine essence/substance) is fully monotheistic, even if that one God exists as three persons. Muslims argue that any claim to three divine persons contradicts strict monotheism, which requires absolute numerical oneness. The disagreement is partly conceptual (what does 'one' mean?) and partly theological (can God's nature be inherently trinitarian?). Christian theologians from Augustine to Aquinas to contemporary scholars have argued that Trinitarian theology maintains true monotheism; Islamic theology rejects this as logically and theologically untenable.

faq.question: What do the Shema and Surah Al-Ikhlas teach about monotheism?
faq.answer: Both passages are understood by their respective traditions as foundational statements of God's oneness. The Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) states 'Hear, O Israel, Yahweh is our God, Yahweh is one'—emphasizing God's unique status as Israel's deity. Surah Al-Ikhlas (112:1) states 'Say, He is Allah, [who is] One'—emphasizing that Allah has no partners, no equals, and no resemblance to creation. Muslims understand both as consistent with pure monotheism and cite them as evidence that the prophets taught Tawhid. Christians affirm both as true while arguing they are consistent with Trinitarian theology—God is uniquely one and has no competitors, which the Trinity preserves by maintaining that the Father, Son, and Spirit all share one divine nature.

faq.question: Did Paul introduce the idea that Jesus was divine?
faq.answer: Biblical scholars debate this extensively. Paul's letters (written before the Gospels) contain highly exalted language about Jesus (Colossians 1:15-17, Philippians 2:6-11) but also affirm monotheism (1 Corinthians 8:4-6). Some scholars argue Paul was the architect of Christian Christology; others argue he was expressing what early Jewish believers already affirmed based on the Resurrection. Islamic scholars typically view Paul as introducing non-Abrahamic theology influenced by Greco-Roman categories. Christian scholars typically view Paul as the apostle most deeply reflecting on what the Resurrection revealed. Christian scholars argue that the evidence shows even before Paul, early disciples were venerating Jesus in ways that would have been radical if he were believed to be merely human; Muslim scholars respond that early enthusiasm and misunderstanding could account for this without implying Jesus actually taught divinity, so scholars disagree on whether this represents continuity with Jesus' own claims or a misunderstanding of his message.

faq.question: How did the Trinity become the official Christian doctrine?
faq.answer: The doctrine of Trinity developed gradually over centuries. Early Christians affirmed the Resurrection, worshipped Jesus, and experienced the Holy Spirit as personal divine presence, but lacked explicit trinitarian vocabulary. The theological crisis came when Christian thinkers had to articulate how Jesus (whom they worshipped) and the Spirit (whom they experienced) related to the one God of Israel. By the Council of Nicaea (325 CE), the church officially affirmed that Jesus (the Son) was of one essence (homoousios) with God (the Father). The full Trinity, including the Holy Spirit, was more fully developed in later creedal statements. Muslims view this as theological innovation and departure from monotheism; Christians view it as organic development of apostolic faith under the Spirit's guidance.

faq.question: What does Quran 5:73 mean, and who was it addressing?
faq.answer: Quran 5:73 states: 'They have certainly disbelieved who say, Allah is the third of three. And there is no god except one God.' Muslims understand this as explicitly rejecting the Christian Trinity as false doctrine. Christians interpret this verse as addressing a heretical or tritheistic form of Christianity—the idea that there are literally three gods, with Allah as one of three distinct beings. Classical Trinitarian theology, in Christian understanding, does not teach that there are three gods or three beings, but one God existing as three persons. However, Muslims argue that any presentation of three divine persons is inherently tritheistic, regardless of the philosophical framing, and thus contradicts the absolute oneness of God taught by all prophets.

furtherReading.title: The Islamic Jesus: How the King of the Jews Became a Prophet of the Muslims
furtherReading.author: Mustafa Akyol
furtherReading.note: A Muslim scholar's examination of Jesus' place in Islamic theology and how Islamic and Christian traditions diverge in understanding Jesus' identity and mission, engaging with historical and textual evidence from both scriptures.

furtherReading.title: Jesus and the Eyewitnesses: The Gospels as Eyewitness Testimony
furtherReading.author: Richard Bauckham
furtherReading.note: A Christian New Testament scholar's examination of the Gospel accounts and early Christian belief in Jesus' resurrection and exaltation, relevant to questions of Christological development.

furtherReading.title: The Fundamentals of Tawheed (Islamic Monotheism)
furtherReading.author: Bilal Philips
furtherReading.note: A foundational Islamic text explaining Tawhid as the core principle of Islam, clarifying what constitutes true monotheism and how it differs from polytheism and theological innovations.

furtherReading.title: The Trinity
furtherReading.author: Rowan Williams
furtherReading.note: A contemporary Christian theologian's examination of Trinitarian doctrine, exploring how the Trinity developed in Christian thought and how it relates to biblical witness, ancient creeds, and the nature of God.
